# SynthPert: Enhancing LLM Biological Reasoning via Synthetic Reasoning Traces for Cellular Perturbation Prediction

**Authors:** Lawrence Phillips, Marc Boubnovski Martell, Aditya Misra, Josefa Lia Stoisser, Cesar A. Prada-Medina, Rory Donovan-Maiye, Kaspar Märtens
**Year:** 2025 (submitted Sep 2025; revised Apr 2026)
**Venue:** arXiv:2509.25346 (cs.AI)

## Abstract
Predicting how cells respond to genetic perturbations is a core systems-biology problem underlying therapeutic discovery and virtual-cell modeling. SynthPert fine-tunes a small open-weights LLM on *synthetic chain-of-thought reasoning traces* generated by a frontier model, teaching biological reasoning rather than just answers. Evaluated on the PerturbQA benchmark, the fine-tuned student model surpasses the frontier teacher that produced its training data, reaches state-of-the-art perturbation-prediction accuracy, generalizes to unseen cell types, and does so with striking data efficiency — even though some distilled reasoning traces are partially inaccurate.

## Key contributions
- Shows synthetic reasoning traces transfer biological understanding effectively — the student beats its teacher — and remain useful even when partly wrong.
- Strong cross-cell-type generalization: 87% accuracy on held-out RPE1 cells.
- Extreme data efficiency: gains preserved using only 2% of quality-filtered training data.
- Unified three-class direction prediction (up / down / not-DE) in one call, rather than sequential binary sub-tasks.

## Methods
The authors fine-tune a **DeepSeek-R1-distilled Llama 3.1 8B** model with **LoRA** on synthetic chain-of-thought traces generated by **OpenAI o4-mini**. PerturbQA reframes perturbation experiments as natural-language tuples `(cell type, perturbation, gene) → {up, down, not differentially expressed}` across K562, RPE1, HepG2, and Jurkat lines (84,550 direction-of-change and 614,479 differential-expression examples). At inference the prompt states whether the gene is differentially expressed and the model selects the direction, predicting the full mechanistic outcome in a single pass instead of chained binary decisions.

## Key results
- Three-class accuracy: **89% overall**, **87%** on unseen RPE1 — vs. base LLM 15% and o4-mini teacher 52%.
- Differential expression AUROC **0.70–0.79** vs. SUMMER baseline 0.58–0.61.
- Direction-of-change AUROC **0.65–0.73** vs. SUMMER 0.62–0.66.
- Three-class F1: not-DE **0.95**, downregulated **0.53**, upregulated **0.22** — upregulated is hardest (0.49 precision / 0.14 recall) due to class imbalance.
- Adding EnrichR pathway analysis *hurt* performance (ΔAUROC −0.07).

## Why it matters for our work
This is the most directly relevant paper for our task: predicting `(perturbed gene, target gene) → up/down/none` for CRISPRi knockdowns. SynthPert validates the whole thesis that biological priors — not memorized train/test overlap — can carry an LLM on perturbation prediction, and it generalizes to *unseen cell types* (87% on held-out RPE1), which is our exact zero-overlap regime. Concrete track implications: **Track A (prompt-only)** — its o4-mini teacher hit only 52% three-class accuracy from prompting alone, a realistic ceiling to beat and a caution that a single call is weak; **Track B (agentic)** — a striking negative result is that bolting on EnrichR pathway tool-use *hurt* AUROC (−0.07), warning us that naive tool augmentation can degrade rather than help; **Track C (fine-tune <10B)** — this is essentially a Track-C recipe: LoRA on an 8B reasoning model trained on synthetic CoT traces from a frontier model beat the teacher using 2% of data, a concrete, budget-compatible blueprint. The reported F1 profile (not-DE 0.95, down 0.53, up 0.22) also mirrors our `none`-is-majority (55%) imbalance and flags upregulation as the class to watch.

## Limitations / open questions
- Severe class imbalance cripples the upregulated class (recall 0.14) — a concern given our `none`-dominant labels.
- External pathway knowledge (EnrichR) degraded performance; why tool/knowledge injection backfires is unresolved (relevant to Track B design).
- Reasoning-trace factuality checked by manual, clause-by-clause review — not scalable; some traces are inaccurate yet still help, so the mechanism of benefit is unclear.
- Results are on PerturbQA cell lines (K562/RPE1/HepG2/Jurkat); transfer to mouse macrophages (our setting) is untested.
